title: The Fate of ‘Wonder Woman 1984’ and Top-Five Gambling Movies
format: Sean and Amanda handicap Warner Bros.' options for 'Wonder Woman 1984' as HBO Max's shaky launch drags on, David Hill of the new Ringer podcast 'Gamblers' joins for three competing top-five gambling-movie lists, and Christopher Landon talks 'Freaky' and a decade-plus inside Blumhouse.

films.title: Wonder Woman 1984
films.note: A theoretical billion-dollar tentpole caught between a weak theatrical market and Warner Bros.' push to make HBO Max matter. Amanda guesses a PVOD/HBO Max combo; Sean bets on HBO Max outright.

films.title: Tenet
films.note: The cautionary tale: under $60 million domestic, the reason Warner Bros. is gun-shy about a straight theatrical release.

films.title: Wonder Woman
films.note: The original, three and a half years earlier, made over $800 million worldwide, and it's that anticipation this sequel is supposedly cashing in on.

films.title: No Time to Die
films.note: The other tentpole stuck in the same Christmas-date standoff; Amanda doubts MGM has the money to send it to streaming.

films.title: Bird Box
films.note: Amanda's evidence that a movie doesn't need to be good to pull 100 million pandemic-bored viewers, though it was free with a Netflix subscription, unlike a $30 to $50 PVOD rental.

segments.heading: David Hill's Top Five Gambling Movies
segments.subject: Gambling movies

films.title: Let It Ride
films.note: No. 1: his pick for the purest gambling movie: no addicts, no violence, just a guy on an unstoppable hot streak at the track.

films.title: The Color of Money
films.note: No. 2: Paul Newman had Walter Tevis write the sequel novel just so he could take another swing at the role that didn't win him an Oscar the first time.

films.title: The Sting
films.note: No. 3: says the con is based on linguist David Maurer's book 'The Big Con,' and Paul Newman's card-handling shots are actually magician Dai Vernon's hands.

films.title: Guys and Dolls
films.note: No. 4: the Damon Runyon adaptation he calls the source of every stylized gambler-speak movie that followed, including Mamet's.

films.title: White Men Can't Jump
films.note: No. 5: a deliberate curveball, his argument that hustling for cash on a playground counts as gambling too.

films.title: Ocean's Eleven
films.note: No. 1: the remake; she admits the gambling is mostly window dressing around a heist, which breaks half of Dave's rules.

films.title: Uncut Gems
films.note: No. 2: the anxiety of it, not the mechanics, is what sticks with her; two hours of Adam Sandler is as close as she wants to get to knowing what gambling feels like.

films.title: Casino Royale
films.note: No. 3: pre-empts the poker nitpicking from Sean and Dave; more interested in what the table stakes do for Bond's character than the cards themselves.

films.title: The Hustler
films.note: No. 4: Paul Newman as, in her words, the patron saint of movie gamblers.

films.title: Indecent Proposal
films.note: No. 5: technically a Vegas movie; she calls out a Janet Maslin review's headline as the whole plot in one line.

films.title: Rounders
films.note: No. 1: credits it with getting him hooked on poker in the first place, and for a hero who's undone by pride rather than addiction or bad luck.

films.title: California Split
films.note: No. 2: Altman's George Segal/Elliott Gould two-hander; he's convinced Mississippi Grind lifted its structure beat for beat.

films.title: Hard Eight
films.note: No. 3: Paul Thomas Anderson's debut, for showing the lifer gambler's downside instead of the winner's arc.

films.title: House of Games
films.note: No. 4: Mamet's directorial debut; a con-artist movie more than a gambling one, per Dave, but he loves the Ricky Jay poker scene regardless.

films.title: The Hustler
films.note: No. 5: his own separate pick of the same film Amanda ranked fourth, which he says should have been higher.

films.title: Freaky
films.note: His writing partner Michael Kennedy pitched it as 'Freaky Friday' meets 'Friday the 13th' under the working title 'Killer Body'; Landon renamed it on the spot. Vince Vaughn was the only actor he considered.

films.title: Happy Death Day
films.note: Sat on a shelf for nearly a decade under earlier titles before Landon revived it; it greenlit over one weekend.

films.title: Psycho
films.note: The first horror movie he remembers seeing, sneaking into his parents' room at age four; the reveal at the end made him scream.

films.title: Another Day in Paradise
films.note: His first produced screenplay, written for Larry Clark, from his self-described emo, edgy-teenager phase.

films.title: Paranormal Activity 2
films.note: His entry point into the Blumhouse/Jason Blum relationship; he had to fill in as director for a day mid-shoot after an on-set emergency.

films.title: Paranormal Activity: The Marked Ones
films.note: The spinoff Blumhouse finally let him direct after years of writing the franchise from the sidelines.
